plan.description: Data Management Plan for project "Engineering Research Infrastructures for European Synergies (ERIES)"
plan.title: DMP - Engineering Research Infrastructures for European Synergies (ERIES)

systemField.label: Description
systemField.description: Briefly describe the context and purpose of the DMP

systemField.label: Language
systemField.description: Select the language of your DMP

referenceField.label: Researchers
referenceField.description: Add here the names of people that have produced, processed, analysed the data described in the DMP.

referenceField.label: Organizations
referenceField.description: Add here the names of the organizations contributing to the creation and revision of the DMPs

referenceField.label: Funding organizations
referenceField.description: Select a funder of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Grants
referenceField.description: Find the grant of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Project
referenceField.description: Projects in Argos are perceived as distinct activities falling under a grant or common activities under different grants in collaborative schemas, eg open call for contributions. Please complete it for the grant associated to your organization if your project falls under this category. In all other cases, please leave blank and it will be autocompleted.

systemField.label: Access Rights
systemField.description: Choose how the DMP is displayed after is published on Zenodo. By choosing Open Access, the DMP will be open on Zenodo after the Publication Date. By choosing Restricted Access, the DMP will be restricted after the publication is made.

referenceField.label: License
referenceField.description: Assign a license to your DMP by selecting the most appropriate from the list.

reference.label: PUBLIC SCIENTIFIC INSTITUTE-INSTITUTE OF EARTHQUAKE ENGINEERING AND ENGINEERING SEISMOLOGY SS CYRIL AND METHODIUS UNIVERSITY SKOPJE IZIIS
CENTRO EUROPEO DI FORMAZIONE E RICERCA IN INGEGNERIA SISMICA
Istituto Universitario Di Studi Superiori Di Pavia
COMMISSARIAT A L ENERGIE ATOMIQUE ET AUX ENERGIES ALTERNATIVES
Centre Scientifique et Technique du Batiment
Engineering Research Infrastructures for European Synergies
Engineering Research Infrastructures for European Synergies (corda_____he::101058684)

description.description:
The dataset is composed of the individual data packets produced via experimental testing activities for each TA project carried out within the Transnational Access (TA) activities of EU-funded project ERIES (Engineering Research Infrastructures for European Synergies). ERIES (https://eries.eu/) provides access to leading experimental facilities in the fields of structural, seismic, wind and geotechnical engineering. The dataset is part of the DMP of the ERIES project, describing the life cycle management of the data produced in TA activities, combined into an integrated framework with open access to external users, satisfying the data management requirements of the EC. Twelve Research Infrastructures (RIs) are participating in ERIES, offering combined and integrated access to the largest collection of high-class experimental installations for earthquake, geotechnical and wind engineering in Europe and Canada. This includes reaction walls, shake tables, wind tunnels, and a Doppler wind LiDAR system, in addition to facilities for integrated studies on geotechnical site effects. The participating RI are:
  • EUCENTRE, Italy
  • STRULAB, University of Patras, Greece
  • EUROSEISTEST-EUROPROTEAS, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, Greece
  • NESDE, LNEC, Portugal
  • TAMARIS, CEA, France
  • Institute of Earthquake Engineering and Engineering Seismology, North Macedonia
  • GS-WinDyn Laboratory, University of Genova, Italy
  • WindEEE, Western University, Canada
  • TU/e-ABLWT, Eindhoven University of Technology, The Netherlands
  • Jules Verne Climatic, CSTB Nantes, France
  • ELSA, Joint Research Centre, Italy
  • SoFSI/EQUALS, University of Bristol, UK
The experimental data offered is a valuable input for diverse stakeholders: researchers; decision-makers; civil protection agencies; technical committees etc. Open access to this data will facilitate research towards: the reduction of losses and disruption due to seismic and wind hazards; the management of their associated risk; the development of innovative solutions to address them that will contribute to a greener and more sustainable society.
description.label: Dataset - Engineering Research Infrastructures for European Synergies (ERIES)

descriptionTemplate.label: Horizon Europe
descriptionTemplate.description: This is the Argos instance of the Horizon Europe template.

selectData.label: Select from the list of options

fieldSet.title: What kind of research output are you describing?
fieldSet.description:
Research data is information (particularly facts or numbers) collected to be examined and considered, and to serve as a basis for reasoning, discussion or calculation. Open access to research data - the right to access and reuse digital research data under the terms and conditions set out in the Grant Agreement.

Other research outputs refer to outputs that are produced or reused during the research and data management lifecycle. They might be objects, instruments and materials in digital form, such as software, workflows, protocols, models, etc. Equally, in their analog form they might include new materials, antibodies, reagents, samples, etc.

fieldSet.title: Is it physical or digital?

labelData.label: Please specify your answer and state the reasons if re-use of any existing data has been considered but discarded.

fieldSet.title: Are you generating or re-using it?
fieldSet.description:
Apart from data produced within your research, you might want to repurpose data that have been produced and shared by others, in different research context.

If you are planning to use other researchers’ data in your research there are a couple of things that you need to take into consideration, such as copyright of datasets, permissions provided through licenses, ethical aspects for reuse, etc. If you are unsure of the license of a dataset that you are re-using, you may use the License Clearance Tool.

Learn more: https://www.openaire.eu/can-i-reuse-someone-else-research-data.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What is the type of the described dataset?
fieldSet.description:
The main distinction between data types is between primary data and secondary data. Data collection may contain both primary and secondary data depending on the source where they have been derived from. 
Primary data is data that have been collected for the first time and have not undergone through data processing and/or analysis, yet. 
Secondary data is data that have been cleaned up, analysed and shared by others (published or unpublished) and they are those that are being typically reused. 
In addition, types of data unveil how they have been collected so they also depend on the process that led to their generation.
For more information, have a look at: https://data.library.virginia.edu/data-management/plan/format-types/

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: What is its format?
fieldSet.description:
Different types of data are acquired, processed and stored (preserved and/or archived) in different ways and can be discipline specific. Many proprietary file formats are “containers” for standard file formats. By packaging them into these containers, a software and/or hardware developer can provide additional functionality, usually by streamlining a process, to analyse data acquired on their platform. However, this has the negative consequence of making these data less interoperable.

You may find some best practices for file formats: https://library.stanford.edu/research/data-management-services/data-best-practices/best-practices-file-formats
For more information about preserving data in appropriate formats: https://www.openaire.eu/data-formats-preservation-guide

labelData.label: Provide the byte size of the dataset.

fieldSet.title: What is its expected size?
fieldSet.description: Give measurable examples per type of output based on common practices in the field. These might be relevant to the volumes of data and how many bytes of storage they occupy, numbers of objects, files, rows, and columns.

selectData.label: Reason of new described dataset collection / generation

labelData.label: Please specify your answer by providing more details on your selection

fieldSet.title: Why are you collecting/generating or re-using it?
fieldSet.description: Data collection is usually at the beginning stages of research data management lifecycles to set the background of what is needed (data generation), what is already there (data reuse) and how to best use it to fulfill the project’s objectives (why). Here you may add information about the scope and objectives of your data collection process.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: You may find a comparison of different data management lifecycles here: Plale, Beth & Kouper, Inna. (2017). The Centrality of Data: Data Lifecycle and Data Pipelines. 10.1016/B978-0-12-809715-1.00004-3.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What is its origin / provenance?

labelData.label: Please specify your answer by providing more context and clear examples

fieldSet.title: To whom might it be useful ('data utility')?

section.description: Beneficiaries should consider which of the questions pertaining to FAIR data as defined in the HE DMP Template, can apply to the management of other research outputs, and should strive to provide sufficient detail on how their research outputs will be managed and shared, or made available for re-use, in line with the FAIR principles.
section.title: Brief description of the described research output

referenceTypeData.label: Select the journal where the publication can be found
Select the literature repository or the pre-print server where the publication can be found

fieldSet.title: Does the described output support any scientific publication?
fieldSet.description: Create the links between scientific publications and research data.

labelData.label: Provide URL with data access / availability statement

fieldSet.title: Is there a data availability statement provided along with the publication?

section.description:
Please complete this section for datasets that are important to validate scientific results described in publications.

Note: The European Commission offers Horizon Europe beneficiaries Open Research Europe (ORE), an open access publishing platform with no publishing fees. ORE is offered as an additional publishing option to Horizon Europe beneficiaries. When ORE is the selected publishing venue, all requirements for open access to scientific publications are fulfilled, as ORE deposits publications in the all-purpose repository Zenodo.
section.title: Publications

referenceTypeData.label: Select the data repository where the dataset can be found

fieldSet.title: Does the described output use or support any published dataset?

labelData.label: Add URL where the software can be found

fieldSet.title: Does the described output use or support any software?

section.description:
In general terms, your research data should be 'FAIR', that is findable, accessible, interoperable and re-usable. These principles precede implementation choices and do not necessarily suggest any specific technology, standard, or implementation-solution. Achieving FAIR data relies equally on the choices you make throughout a research process, but also on providers whose services enable FAIRness of data to be met. 

Learn more about the FAIR principles: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4792175/ 

Learn how you can comply with FAIR principles: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-make-your-data-fair
section.title: Making data and other outputs findable, including provisions for metadata

selectData.label: Please select the used Data Identifier System
Please select the used Researchers' Identifier System
Please select the used Organizations' Identifier System
Please select the used Projects' Identifier System

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: What type(s) of persistent identifier(s) are used for the described dataset / output?
fieldSet.description: Persistent and/or Permanent Identifiers (PIDs) uniquely identify objects, people, organisations and activities and can ensure that the scientific output is accessible even when the URL of the website has changed. PIDs can be assigned to research outputs including publications, data and software/code. PIDs can also be assigned to researchers, samples, organisations and projects. A PID may be connected to a metadata record describing an item rather than the item itself. PIDs are usually provided by data repositories and other deposit platforms. Re3data includes tags to show which platforms that it indexes assign PIDs to their content.

uploadData.label: If desired, upload an image of the used schema

labelData.label: Provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Will you provide metadata for the described dataset / output?
fieldSet.description: Metadata is data about data and is an essential set of information describing scientific outputs, in the form of either physical or digital objects, in a machine-readable format. According to the expected use, metadata can be given different attributes. Most common type which enables discovery and identification are descriptive metadata. Descriptive metadata contain information about key aspects needed to search for and successfully find a given scientific output, e.g. by its title, author/creator, abstract, keywords. Moreover, metadata may be used for describing a service or a scientific instrument.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What type(s) of metadata?
fieldSet.description: Please outline what type of metadata will be created and how.

referenceTypeData.label: Select a vocabulary from the list

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually

fieldSet.title: Do the metadata use standardised vocabularies?
fieldSet.description: Standardised vocabularies enable greater interoperability across systems. There are generic and discipline specific metadata standards which have been compiled so as to contain vital information that enable exchange while taking into consideration specificities of work and demands in different disciplines, even in different areas of work within a discipline.

labelData.label: URL where the vocabularies of the data can be found
Briefly describe selected vocabularies application for your needs

fieldSet.title: Please provide URL/Description of used vocabularies
Are the metadata searchable?
fieldSet.description: Searchable metadata are metadata indexed by search engines and are identifiable by web crawlers.

selectData.label: Metadata are indexed in/ by a

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: How are searchable metadata provided?

labelData.label: Please provide more detail for your answer

fieldSet.title: Are keywords provided in the metadata?
fieldSet.description: Keywords and tags help to optimize the possibility for discovery and then potential re-use of data.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Are metadata harvestable?
fieldSet.description: Usually, this is facilitated by services that apply standard protocols for information exchange. For example, in repositories, metadata exchange is supported by the OAI-PMH protocol.

section.description: Data are findable when described with metadata and vocabularies in a standardized way, assigned Persistent Identifiers (PIDs) and are registered or indexed in a searchable resource.
section.title: Making data findable, including provisions for metadata

section.description: Not all data can be made publicly open, hence data can be FAIR but not open, or open but not FAIR or both FAIR and open. Data are accessible when uploaded in a data repository and retrieved by their PIDs. When data can not be shared openly, metadata should be provided (even when the data are no longer available). In the case of sensitive or personal data, anonymization or pseudonymization and specific access rights can be applied. Where accessing data requires the use of complementary methods or tools, such procedures should be documented.
section.title: Making data and other outputs openly accessible

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert the data repository
Add URL of the system hosting a distribution of a dataset.
Add host description

fieldSet.title: In which repository will the dataset / output be deposited?
fieldSet.description:
A data repository, otherwise known as a data archive, is an online collection of datasets that are described and classified in a standard way that makes data discovery and retrieval easier to be performed by both humans and machines. 

For more information on the processes for datasets, you can have a look at https://www.openaire.eu/rdm-handbook

selectData.label: What processes does it make use of?

options.label: Has an open access content policy
options.value: Has an open access content policy

options.label: Provides Open Access content (free at the point of use)
options.value: Provides Open Access content (free at the point of use)

options.label: Supports authentication and authorization of users
options.value: Supports authentication and authorization of users

options.label: Has data security mechanisms in place
options.value: Has data security mechanisms in place

fieldSet.title: Is the selected repository a trusted source?
fieldSet.description:
Trusted repositories assume a central role in the Horizon Europe for the deposition of and access to publications and research data. They are: 

  • Certified repositories (e.g. CoreTrustSeal, nestor Seal DIN31644, ISO16363) or disciplinary and domain repositories commonly used and endorsed by the research communities. Such repositories should be recognised internationally.
  • General-purpose repositories or institutional repositories that present the essential characteristics of trusted repositories, i.e.: 
    • display specific characteristics of organisational, technical and procedural quality such as services, mechanisms and/or provisions that are intended to secure the integrity and authenticity of their contents, thus facilitating their use and re-use in the short- and long-term. Trusted repositories have specific provisions in place and offer explicit information online about their policies, which define their services (e.g. acquisition, access, security of content, long-term sustainability of service including funding etc.).
    • provide broad, equitable and ideally open access to content free at the point of use, as appropriate, and respect applicable legal and ethical limitations. They assign persistent unique identifiers to contents (e.g. DOIs, handles, etc.), such that the contents (publications, data and other research outputs) are unequivocally referenced and thus citeable. They ensure that contents are accompanied by metadata sufficiently detailed and of sufficiently high quality to enable discovery, reuse and citation and contain information about provenance and licensing; metadata are machine- actionable and standardized (e.g. Dublin Core, Data Cite etc.) preferably using common non-proprietary formats and following the standards of the respective community the repository serves, where applicable.
    • facilitate mid- and long-term preservation of the deposited material. They have mechanisms or provisions for expert curation and quality assurance for the accuracy and integrity of datasets and metadata, as well as procedures to liaise with depositors where issues are detected. They meet generally accepted international and national criteria for security to prevent unauthorized access and release of content and have different levels of security depending on the sensitivity of the data being deposited to maintain privacy and confidentiality.

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert the name of certification(s)

fieldSet.title: Add certification(s)
fieldSet.description: Repository certified to a standard.

fieldSet.title: Add appropriate arrangements made with the repository(ies) where the described dataset will be deposited
Does the repository(ies) assign datasets / outputs with persistent identifiers?
fieldSet.description: Persistent identifiers (PIDs) are key in ensuring the findability of research outputs, including data. They are globally unique and long-lasting references to digital objects (such as data, publications and other research outputs) or non-digital objects such as researchers, research institutions, grants, etc. Frequently used persistent identifiers include digital object identifiers (DOIs), Handles, and others. For further reading on PID types, please refer to https://www.dpconline.org/handbook/technical-solutions-andtools/persistent-identifiers.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Does the repository(ies) resolve the identifiers to a digital object?
Does the repository support versioning?

labelData.label: Provide your answer

fieldSet.title: What is the described dataset / output title?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: How is the dataset / output shared?
fieldSet.description: Indicates access mode for data. If certain datasets cannot be shared (or need to be shared under restricted access conditions), explain why, clearly separating legal and contractual reasons from intentional restrictions. Note that in multi-beneficiary projects it is also possible for specific beneficiaries to keep their data closed if opening their data goes against their legitimate interests or other constraints as per the Grant Agreement.

fieldSet.title: What is the reason of limiting access to the dataset / output?
fieldSet.description:
Explain why the described dataset or parts of its distribution cannot be shared (or need to be shared under restricted access conditions),clearly separating legal and contractual reasons from intentional restrictions. Note that in multi-beneficiary projects it is also possible for specific beneficiaries to keep their data closed if opening their data goes against their legitimate interests or other constraints as per the Grant Agreement.

Embargo periods are tied to sharing and publishing scientific outputs. An embargo might be applied to give time to publish or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents). If an embargo is applied to give time to publish or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents), specify why and how long this will apply, bearing in mind that research data should be made available as soon as possible.

labelData.label: Due date of effect of the embargo

fieldSet.title: If an embargo applies, please specify when the dataset / output will be made available.
fieldSet.description: If an embargo is applied to give time to publish or seek protection of the intellectual property (e.g. patents), specify why and how long this will apply, bearing in mind that research data should be made available as soon as possible.

referenceTypeData.label: Select EOSC service needed to access the dataset

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add tools names

fieldSet.title: Are there any methods or tools required to access the dataset / output?
fieldSet.description: Describe processes needed to query and access data. These might be relevant to SPARQL access points, visualizer software, download, etc. Data access controls, such as passwords or firewalls, should be used to limit access to confidential data and protect them from unauthorised changes.

labelData.label: Briefly explain the method(s) need to be applied for data access
URL where the method can be found

fieldSet.title: Please provide information about the method(s) needed to access the dataset / output.

labelData.label: Briefly describe the tool needed to be used for data access
Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add used tool

fieldSet.title: Please provide information about the tools needed to access the dataset / output.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Is the described dataset / output supported by a data access committee?
fieldSet.description: Explain if there is a need for a dedicated data access committee to evaluate/approve access requests to personal/sensitive data, etc.

labelData.label: Please provide details on your strategy
Please specify how the identity of the person accessing the data will be ascertained

fieldSet.title: Please specify how the dataset / output will be accessed during and after the project ends

labelData.label: Please specify the time period

fieldSet.title: Please specify how long after the project has ended the dataset / output will be made accessible for
fieldSet.description: Please check with funders' or institutions's policies which might apply an embargo on data access.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Will you provide metadata even if the described dataset / output can not be openly shared?

labelData.label: Creative Commons Zero (CC0)
Other
Please specify the use of other license

fieldSet.title: Under which license will metadata be provided?
fieldSet.description: According to the Grant Agreement, metadata are made openly available and licensed under a public domain dedication CC0.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Do metadata provide information about how to access the described dataset / output?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Will metadata remain available after the dataset / output is no longer available?

section.description: To enhance the findability of research outputs, and their potential reuse, standardised metadata frameworks are essential, ensuring that data and other research outputs are accompanied by rich metadata that provides them with context.
section.title: Metadata

referenceTypeData.label: Select a vocabulary from the list

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add vocabulary

fieldSet.title: Does your (meta)data use a controlled vocabulary?
fieldSet.description: Controlled vocabularies provide standard terminology as opposed to keywords or tags used to classify information. Examples: taxonomies, ontologies, thesauri.

labelData.label: Please specify

uploadData.label: If desired, upload an image of the schema created

fieldSet.title: If you created the vocabulary, where can it be found?
fieldSet.description: Consider openly publishing the generated ontologies or vocabularies to allow reusing, refining or extending them.

referenceTypeData.label: Select a metadata standard from the list

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add used metadata standard(s)

fieldSet.title: Have you applied a standard schema for your (meta)data?
fieldSet.description: You may browse the Metadata Standards Catalog here: http://rd-alliance.github.io/metadata-directory/

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Will you provide a mapping to more commonly used ontologies?

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What is the methodology followed?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: What community-endorsed interoperability best practices are followed?

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: Does the described dataset / output provide qualified references with other outputs?
fieldSet.description: A qualified reference is a cross-reference that explains its intent. For example, X is regulator of Y is a much more qualified reference than X is associated with Y, or X see also Y. The goal therefore is to create as many meaningful links as possible between (meta)data resources to enrich the contextual knowledge about the data. (Source: https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/i3-metadata-include-qualified-references-metadata/)

section.description: Data are interoperable, meaning they can be easily understood and shared with other platforms and systems, when they are created using standard vocabularies and include references to other data and metadata.
section.title: Making data and other outputs interoperable

labelData.label: If an embargo applies, please specify when the dataset will be made openly available
Provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What internationally recognised licence will you use for your dataset / output?
fieldSet.description:
There are a number of licenses that can be assigned to your research outputs. There are different licenses for software and for publications and data. For the latter, a common practice is the use of Creative Commons licenses which are machine readable.

For more information on the licences required for data under Horizon Europe, please refer to the AGA (article 17).

You may learn more about how to choose a license here: https://www.openaire.eu/how-do-i-license-my-research-data
You may use the License Clearance Tool (LCT) to check compliance of licenses for derivative works: https://lct.ni4os.eu/

labelData.label: Please specify your answer

fieldSet.title: What reusability and / or reproducibility methods are followed?
fieldSet.description: Provide supplementary material that validate data analysis and facilitate data re-use.

fieldSet.title: Will you provide the described dataset / output in the public domain?

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Do you intend to ensure (re)use by third parties after your project finishes?
fieldSet.description: Despite where data are stored for everyday activities during the project lifetime, there are certain options to choose from data storage that ensures reuse in the log run. Most popular are data archives, otherwise found as repositories. Depending of data volume, archiving might be subject to small fees.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: Is provenance well documented?
fieldSet.description: Data provenance, or data lineage, is a type of metadata that tracks digital object's history in time and its relations with other entities. You may also check: https://casrai.org/term/provenance-metadata/

options.label: Set up of scientific and technical committee
options.value: Set up of scientific and technical committee

options.label: Use of tools for automatic checks
options.value: Use of tools for automatic checks

options.label: Consistency verified with data models and standards
options.value: Consistency verified with data models and standards

labelData.label: Provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: What documented procedures for quality assurance do you have in place?
fieldSet.description:
Quality assurance procedures are closely related to RDM and are meant to validate that data are clean (no duplicates or inconsistencies), error-free, well structured and represented in analysis. Quality assurance checks can be facilitated by the use of tools or scripts.

To familiarise yourself with the process, you may find a checklist here: https://www.eur.nl/sites/corporate/files/2017-11/Checklist_QA_QC_RDM_0_2_MD_20150129.pdf

section.description: Data can be reused when the conditions about how others can make use of the data are well-described following community-standards and are communicated as specified by the owners. Such information can be found in licenses attributed to data and in references about the data provenance.
section.title: Increasing data and other outputs reuse

labelData.label: Add value
Add name other RDM activity associated with the cost
Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: What will be the cost of making the described output FAIR?
fieldSet.description: To understand what costs are allocated across the steps of a RDM lifecycle, how to estimate them and how to prevent them, please check: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-comply-to-h2020-mandates-rdm-costs

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answer

fieldSet.title: How will this cost be covered?
fieldSet.description: Note that costs related to research data/output management are eligible as part of the Horizon Europe grant (if compliant with the Grant Agreement conditions).

referenceTypeData.label: Search by using ORCIDs of people responsible for data management

labelData.label: Couldn't find it? Insert it manually
Add name
Please specify the role

fieldSet.title: Identify the people who will be responsible and their role(s) in the management of the described output
fieldSet.description:
Provide names and responsibilities of researchers’ or data managers’ data management and
stewardship activities that are performed throughout the project for the described output. Follow the research data
management and software management lifecycles to guide your steps in data capture, software development,, metadata production, data quality,
storage and backup, data and software archiving and sharing.

section.description: Data management can be costly, especially when its planning hasn't been sufficient from the very beginning of the research process. Costing of data management includes for example potential use of proprietary services and tools or extra effort needed to perform specific tasks or even to develop tools from scratch.
section.title: Allocation of resources

labelData.label: Please specify your selection

fieldSet.title: What security measures are followed?
fieldSet.description: Describe what provisions are in place for data security.

selectData.label: Select from the list of answers

labelData.label: Please specify your answer

fieldSet.title: What conditions do the security measures meet?
fieldSet.description: Describe data provisions, including data recovery as well as secure storage/archiving and transfer of sensitive data.

labelData.label: Please specify

fieldSet.title: How will you preserve the described dataset / output in the long term?
fieldSet.description: Please describe curation and preservation policies followed for repository content.

labelData.label: Please provide more details for your answers
Indicate (URL) where a protocol from a meeting with an ethical committee can be found.

fieldSet.title: Are there any ethical or legal issues that can have an impact on sharing the described dataset / output?
Does the described dataset / output contain sensitive information?
Does the described dataset / output contain personal data?

visibilityRule.value: Data accompanied by informed consent statements

options.label: Privacy constraints and applicable ethical norms
options.value: Privacy constraints and applicable ethical norms

options.label: Data accompanied by informed consent statements
options.value: Data accompanied by informed consent statements

selectData.label: Will informed consent for data sharing and long term preservation be included in questionnaires dealing with personal data?

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: What are the methods used for processing and accessing sensitive/personal information?
Do you make use of other procedures for data management?
fieldSet.description: Please check: https://www.scienceeurope.org/our-resources/guidance-document-presenting-a-framework-for-discipline-specific-research-data-management, https://www.dtls.nl/fair-data/research-data-management/data-management-knowledge-tools/

labelData.label: Please provide links (URLs) and additional information on your answer

fieldSet.title: Documentation of other procedures

field.textValue: For each experimental campaign carried out by a lab in ERIES and at the responsibility of the official lab representative (mentioned in section 4.1.3), all information (metadata and data) will be stored to a public repository and a Digital Object Identifier (DOI) will be issued. Many repositories (e.g., Zenodo, https://zenodo.org/) provide the DOI free-of-charge. As described in later sections of this document, other data repositories may also be used, provided – among others – that a DOI is issued for each data packet.

File collections containing a large number of files or subfolders will be published, as uncompressed *.zip files or *.tar files: these are standardised formats which can be decompressed in the long term. As the format of the files within these container files must be also usable in the long term, non-proprietary or open standard file formats will be employed. Such file formats for numerical valued data include: plain text files (*.txt, *.c,*,h, *.py, *.cpp, etc., coded as ASCII, UTF-8 or UTF-16 using byte order mark) and comma-delimited text files (*.csv). For explanatory documents, PDF/A (preferred subtypes 2b and 2u) or .txt files can be used. For images, TIFF (*.tif) uncompressed, portable network graphics (*.png) or JPEG2000 (*.jp2, lossless compression), will be employed. For audio and video files, WAV (*.wav, uncompressed pulse-code modulated) and FFV1 codec in (ver. 3.0 or later, in Matroska, *.mkv, container), respectively, will be used.

The data produced under the TA activities of the ERIES project will be openly shared. In case other experimental data is produced (e.g. after the end of the project) by a research infrastructure (member or not of the ERIES consortium) in the fields of structural, seismic, wind and geotechnical engineering, the data may be offered with restricted access. In such a case, the Access Portal developed under WP3 of the project, will provide metadata, even if the data are not openly shared.

The RIs will, before uploading research data, consult with the repository to guarantee that their data will be treated in the most secure manner possible to protect them from various types of malicious attacks. In addition, they will ensure that the repository of their choice keeps both data files and metadata in multiple online and independent replicas. Lastly, they will ensure that the uploaded data comply with applicable laws, including, but not limited to, privacy, data protection, and intellectual property rights - sensitive personal data will either be anonymized to an appropriate degree or fully consent cleared.

The metadata structure – under development in WP3 of ERIES project – uses the Common European Research Information Format (CERIF) schema for the general part of the metadata. A domain-specific part of the metadata will also be provided to give more information on the structure of the data and increase data discovery, reuse and interoperability. The Exchange Data Format, EDF (compatible with Data Catalogue vocabulary, DCAT-AP, classes) developed in the SERIES project, has been used and will continue to form the standard data model used for the earthquake (and, partially, for geotechnical) engineering metadata - it will now be complemented with the GEM Taxonomy. The “Deliverable D7.1- Standard for site condition metadata” developed in the SERA project will be used for additional geotechnical engineering metadata, if necessary. For wind engineering data, an expanded version of the EDF will be developed in ERIES.

The experimental data offered is a valuable input for diverse stakeholders: researchers; decision-makers; governmental and civil protection authorities; construction and consulting companies; technical committees, including the code committees responsible for the development and updating of the Eurocodes or other technical standards. Experimental testing of structural components or structures under accidental actions, like earthquakes and winds, produces a large amount of recorded data. Large-scale testing and/or testing with advanced experimental techniques, such as shake table testing, hybrid simulation etc., can only be performed in a few experimental facilities worldwide. Such tests have always produced very valuable scientific knowledge on the behaviour of structural components and structures, improving procedures and Structural Code provisions for designing safer new structures, as well as for assessing the risk of old structures to various hazards. Open access to the large amount of data produced by experimental activities of this type is indeed valuable as it is an ignitor of research targeted to the reduction of losses and disruption owing to seismic and wind hazards, leading to effective management of the associated risk. Furthermore, the use/reuse of data paves the way for developing innovative solutions addressing the problems at hand and contributing to a "greener" and more sustainable engineering future in Europe.
Exchange Data Format (EDF)

Repositories do not assume responsibilities regarding the duration of the availability of the data in the future. Zenodo indicates a duration of 20 years and that: "In the highly unlikely event that Zenodo will have to close operations, we guarantee that we will migrate all content to other suitable repositories, and since all uploads have DOIs, all citations and links to Zenodo resources (such as your data) will not be affected " (https://help.zenodo.org/).

Data will be produced by experimental testing activities within the TA projects of EU-funded project ERIES. The twelve-strong group RIs in ERIES offer access to high-class experimental installations for earthquake, geotechnical and wind engineering in Europe and Canada. This includes reaction walls, shake tables, wind tunnels, and a Doppler wind LiDAR system, in addition to facilities for integrated studies on geotechnical site effects. The participating RI are:
  • EUCENTRE, Italy
  • STRULAB, University of Patras, Greece
  • EUROSEISTEST-EUROPROTEAS, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, Greece
  • NESDE, LNEC, Portugal
  • TAMARIS, CEA, France
  • Institute of Earthquake Engineering and Engineering Seismology, North Macedonia
  • GS-WinDyn Laboratory, University of Genova, Italy
  • WindEEE, Western University, Canada
  • TU/e-ABLWT, Eindhoven University of Technology, The Netherlands
  • Jules Verne Climatic, CSTB Nantes, France
  • ELSA, Joint Research Centre, Italy
  • SoFSI/EQUALS, University of Bristol, UK

Curated (processed) data from tests (structural/geotechnical, for earthquake type of excitation or a quasi-static) sum up to about 2GB per test uploaded (includes numerical data, pictures and video). 57 tests of this type are estimated to take place within the TA activities of the ERIES project. Curated (processed) data from tests under wind action range between 1GB and 100GB (comprising tests with Lidar and Particle Image Velocimetry, PIV, techniques). 40 tests of this type are expected to take place in ERIES.

As described in other parts of this document, there is flexibility for a data provider to choose the data repository of their preference, provided – among others – that a DOI is issued for each data packet produced under a certain experimental campaign (i.e., one DOI per TA project of ERIES). In case the all-purpose repository Zenodo (https://zenodo.org/) is used as a data repository, a - free of charge - DOI is automatically issued. If a data provider chooses Gitlab, a DOI is again issued, this time not directly by Gitlab, but through Zenodo.
In case that a different repository is used (e.g., a National Infrastructure), the data providing Research Infrastructure of ERIES, respecting the relevant clauses of the Grant Agreement it has signed, will make sure that a DOI is issued. The DOI is also (technically) a mandatory prerequisite for a partner to be able to include the metadata of each data packet in the ERIES platform.

New experimental data in the fields of structural, seismic, wind and geotechnical engineering produced under the TA activities of EU-funded project ERIES. More specifically, the data will be produced by the experimental testing activities of the partner laboratories in ERIES for the sake of user groups and will comprise curated (processed) data of all measurements acquired during testing, including specimen details, pictures, and videos.

textListValues.textListValue: Has an open access content policy
Provides Open Access content (free at the point of use)
Has data security mechanisms in place

field.textValue: An embargo may be applied to provide time to ERIES TA user groups to publish the research performed and present the data acquired (journals, conferences, etc.). The TA User Agreement between an ERIES Research Infrastructure and a User Group foresees the possibility of an embargo period set by default to 6 months.

For all data packets that will have been stored in a cloud-based repository, the Access Portal will provide and store a complete set of metadata. The metadata will also accompany each data packet in the data repository where the latter will be stored. The repository will be selected upon it providing credible proof of the long-term preservation of the data. An EU member state governmental repository may be opted for, or Zenodo. Moreover, the publication of a data paper for each TA project will significantly increase the longevity of the metadata. Data papers (with accompanying metadata) may be published in peer-reviewed journals, which are expected to preserve the data papers for a very long period. Options in this direction include EC's open-access publishing platform Open Research Europe (ORE), and journals, like "Data in Brief" and "Journal of Structural Engineering (JSE)" of the American Society of Civil Engineering (ASCE).
URL will be available when the paper(s) of each TA project will be published.
Experimental Test Data - Engineering Research Infrastructures for European Synergies (ERIES)

Data packets produced in ERIES are self-contained - they constitute the complete outcome of a specific User Project in ERIES and are fully described by the associated metadata - the latter are stored in three different places: in the (re-built version of) ERIES data portal (to facilitate discovery by users), in a trusted repository in unison with the data and finally, via the publication of a Data Paper in a peer-reviewed journal such as "Data in Brief" or "Journal of Structural Engineering (JSE/ASCE)" or the EC's open access publishing platform Open Research Europe (ORE). Proper citation of the data is ensured by the citation text provided for each data packet at the ERIES Data Portal - the same citation text is provided additionally at the repository where data resides.

Each laboratory is responsible for providing free access to the data produced in Transnational Access activities of ERIES project by depositing data and associated metadata to trusted repositories. In recognition of the importance of the data to the scientific community, data will be available as open access even after the project finishes - this is reflected in the licensing scheme selected for ERIES data, i.e. CC-BY 4.0.
We are using Exchange Data Format (EDF), which complies to DCAT-AP Class "5.3 Dataset"

Rich metadata will be provided to facilitate discovery, identification, detailed description of the data produced. WP3 (“Refactoring and expansion of SERIES open access database - Integration of services to EOSC”) of ERIES project works towards this direction to provide a platform facilitating the FAIR-ness (findable, accessible, interoperable, re-usable) of data.
Several facilities of the consortium were the first (SERIES project, 2009) to establish a common data format for structural tests and accompanied metadata - to that end, they built an ensemble of distributed databases communicating their data to a central, user access, portal (DAP, www.dap.series.upatras.gr), to openly provide the data/metadata produced under their TA activity.
In the re-factored access portal in ERIES, compliance of the general part of ERIES-metadata (responsible, facility, funding agency, repository in which the data reside, DOI of the data packet in the repository, etc.) to the CERIF metadata model will be ensured (as done in SERIES-SERA project database).
In the absence of a widely accepted, domain-specific metadata model both for data from earthquake testing campaigns and from wind engineering counterparts, the consortium will develop such a model for both domains and will upload all necessary information, so that the structure of the data provided will be understandable by users. The Exchange Data Format (EDF) data/metadata model developed by the consortium in the past (SERIES-SERA projects) will form the basis for such development regarding data from seismic testing activities, taking also into consideration the GEM taxonomy (www.globalquakemodel.org/gempublications/GEM-building-taxonomy-version-2.0) used for the description of buildings for the Global Earthquake Model.
The full package of the metadata, as stored in the Access Portal, will also accompany the data packet in the data repository where the latter will be stored.

A scheme for opening both data and metadata to users has been set forth. The metadata of an experimental campaign will be made openly available on the ERIES platform soon upon completion of the testing campaign (the exact period is defined in the TA User Agreement).
The research data itself will be made openly available as soon as possible. An embargo may apply to the data itself, in order to provide time to the user group that proposed the test to publish the results. The maximum duration of the embargo period - as agreed through the TA User Agreement signed by facilities and user groups – is set by default to 6 months.

There is a cost for each partner for: (a) data curation and uploading to the open access database according to the requirements of the Grant Agreement and the Data Management Plan, (b) creating an EDF-spinoff metadata schema for wind engineering data and, (c) creation of the Access Portal and the rest of the activities of WP3: “Refactoring and expansion of SERIES open access database - Integration of services to EOSC”.
The lump sum of the above cost is 430,000€ (direct costs) and are included in the ERIES’ budget with the exception of the University of Bristol (UK) which is self-funded as per the Grant Agreement. There may be an additional cost (born by the lab) for the data repository chosen. Each RI can choose the data repository of its preference (Zenodo, Gitlab, other), respecting the requirements of the DMP. The cost of this service depends on the data repository’s policy. Data repositories providing large storage space free of charge will be preferred (e.g., Zenodo or a national/institutional infrastructure).
Direct cost

The repositories selected by the partners to store the data from the tests at the respective facilities will be chosen so as that they are open, accessible, respect the principles of EOSC and guarantee longevity of the data. The repositories indicated in 3.2.1.1 are well established public repositories, no special arrangements need to be made for depositing data to them.
The open access repositories where the data will be stored do not require such identification for open access data.

Upon completion of an experimental campaign (completion of the TA User Agreement), the access-providing RI and the User Group have the responsibility (on the basis of a signed TA User Agreement) to upload metadata within a period defined in TA User Agreement. Data will be deposited with open access in a trusted repository following an embargo period set by default to 6 months (if such is agreed),.
After the project ends, data will continue being openly available, since it will be required to have been uploaded to an accredited repository, it will have a DOI, and the repository will be able to maintain the data in the long term (Zenodo guarantees a period of 20 years).

The dataset includes new research data produced under the TA projects of EU-funded project ERIES in the fields of structural, seismic, wind and geotechnical engineering. Open access to experimental measurements, specimen details, photos and videos recorded during tests in the framework of ERIES’ TA activities is provided. The experimental installations producing and offering the data include reaction walls; shake tables; wind tunnels; a Doppler wind LiDAR system; facilities for integrated studies on geotechnical site effects.
New

ERIES comprises different types of experimental facilities and such practices cannot be adopted. The “Exchange Data Format (EDF)” (compatible with Data Catalogue vocabulary, DCAT-AP, and classes) developed in the SERIES project, will continue to form the standard data model, helping to keep and present the data in a unified way. Interoperability will be facilitated by adopting standard formats and units of measure widely used in the community and by proper documentation in terms of Readme files. The Readme files will be particularly important for tests that may not be covered sufficiently by the metadata info (due to the large variety of the tests that can be performed in the Research Infrastructures participating in ERIES, the metadata scheme - to be adopted - may not accurately describe every test).
OTHER: e.g. specimen geometry definition and sensor positions via Autocad drawings

Security measures for the uploaded data are those of the repository where they reside.
Thus, as a precaution, only trusted repositories providing increased security may be used, such as Zenodo. National/Institutional repositories normally provide increased security, nevertheless, partners are advised to consult with the repository first.

The domain-specific vocabulary to be used for earthquake engineering test data will comprise a combination of the ad-hoc Exchange Data Format (i.e. EDF, from SERIES and SERA EU-funded projects) and the GEM taxonomy (www.globalquakemodel.org/gempublications/GEM-building-taxonomy-version-2.0). Details on both glossaries will be available on the ERIES Data Portal.
For the geotechnical engineering test data, the description of the site condition metadata, as included in the European Geotechnical Database (a Europe-wide geotechnical, geological and site conditions inventory developed in the SERA project via interaction with EPOS), will be employed. Additional terms may have to be developed based on the needs of the specific User Group tests which will be performed in ERIES.
For wind engineering test data, an expanded version of the EDF will be used for the needs of test campaigns in ERIES.

As explained in point 4.1.1 above, an estimated cost of 415.000€ is foreseen in ERIES’ budget for data management, which covers the development of the Data Access Portal and all other activities of WP3 “Refactoring and expansion of SERIES open access database - Integration of services to EOSC”. It also covers data curation and uploading by each partner. Any possible additional cost for the data repository should be covered by the partner.

Experimental data produced under experimental activities in large RIs in the fields of structural, earthquake, geotechnical and wind engineering is of great value to the corresponding scientific communities: they result out of large-scale, labour-intensive, high-cost, time-consuming and technically demanding structural/geotechnical/wind engineering tests at highly specialized facilities. Open access to such experimental data will be offered with the aim of sharing this valuable information with researchers worldwide, providing data for the calibration of numerical models, creating knowledge useful for the safety of the public and forming the basis of decisions by policy-makers, keeping track of experimental activities performed (among others, this will help avoid duplications of experimental campaigns) and to combine the present data with other experimental data available elsewhere, facilitating comparison and further research in the above fields.

Roles indicated in parenthesis above
DMC: Data Management Committee

Gerard O’Reilly (DMC/IUSS/EUCENTRE), Gian Michele Calvi (IUSS/EUCENTRE), Fabio Germagnoli (EUCENTRE), Stathis Bousias (DMC/UPAT), Kyriazis Pitilakis (AUTH), Paulo Candeias (LNEC), Darius Seyedi (CEA), Anastasios Sextos (UBRI), Aleksandra Bogdanovic (IZIIS), Maria Pia Repetto (UNIGE), Girma Bitsuamlak (WU), Stefanie Gillmeier (TUE), Philippe Delpech (CSTB), Georgios Tsionis (JRC)

textListValues.textListValue: Consistency verified with data models and standards

field.textValue:
The first step towards quality assurance is taken when the Research Infrastructures upload the metadata of a research campaign to the Access Portal:
  1. The person introducing the metadata will have to be one endorsed by the Research Infrastructure – where available login via the EOSC Authentication and Authorization Infrastructure will be used in the ERIES Data Portal. For non-European ERIES lab partners, an ad-hoc mechanism will be used,
  2. The Exchange Data Format will be used for entering the metadata, with relevant guidance being presented (via tooltips) during inputting,
  3. The front-end of the software used to insert the metadata will be carefully designed by the specialized team in ERIES (Human-Computer Interaction group), with checks for clarity, completeness, consistency, duplicates, adherence to EDF model, etc. It is reminded that the same model, i.e. the EDF, will be used for the metadata of all experimental campaigns in ERIES, thus guaranteeing uniformity,
  4. Completeness of information provided shall be checked, to the extent possible,
  5. Metadata without a DOI on the data and a link to the repository where they have been stored, will be considered as "provisional" and will remain in "draft" mode until this information is provided,
  6. No direct storage of the metadata will be allowed - the information provided will remain in "draft" mode for a limited period of time, during which modifications will be possible. A report-type summary of the metadata will be generated, for further checking by the leader of the User Group which proposed the specific TA project
  7. After the metadata become public, no changes can be applied without the knowledge and consent of the Data Management Committee,
  8. Upon completion of the metadata entry process, the representative of the RI will confirm the metadata and will finalize the entry process - the metadata are then openly available to the Access Portal and a (downloadable) report, summing up all the information provided, will be generated.
  9. When metadata is finalized, a downloadable archive with the metadata will be created to be stored in the repository in unison with the data files.
Regarding the curated data to be uploaded to a repository, the following steps will be taken for quality assurance:
  1. Research Infrastructures (RΙs) participating in ERIES form an ensemble of the most advanced facilities in Europe in the areas of structural, geotechnical and wind engineering testing, with an established reputation in the relevant fields and long-standing experience. In recognition of their capacity (cf. TA User Agreement), the responsibility for data processing and uploading to a repository is entrusted with the RI,
  2. The ERIES Data Portal will include a form for user-reported issues which have been identified with respect to the understanding of the metadata/data. Comments and/or queries of a user on the metadata/data of a certain test will be automatically addressed to the representative of the RI that performed the test. Since the repositories selected for metadata/data depositing should be selected so as to support versioning, eventual oversights can be easily corrected, and a new version be uploaded.
We are using Exchange Data Format (EDF), which complies to DCAT-AP Class "5.3 Dataset"

The data to be uploaded are recorded data and become known for the first time. Additionally, it will be required that the trusted repository selected by the data-providing facilities will support versioning. Consequently, the DOI, in unison with the version, provide adequate info regarding data lineage.

Each RI is responsible for providing free access to the data it produced during ERIES and for preserving it beyond. While data availability within the duration of ERIES is guaranteed, data longevity after the completion of the project is affected by the cost of maintaining them and the operation of the repository in the long run. Striving to satisfy the former requirement, the RIs should opt for free-of-charge data storage, thus increasing the chances that data will be available beyond the umbrella of ERIES. To fulfil the second requirement, RIs will explore the commitments different repositories make regarding long-term data preservation. Indicatively, the Zenodo repository offers a 20-year period of operation (at least).
A second option that will be explored by the RIs regards the use of well-established, national/institutional repositories which, where/if available, may offer low- or no-cost for data storage and reliable long-term operation.
On the other hand, the metadata is much smaller in size; it can easily remain available even after a set of data may no longer be available. Because of their small volume, metadata residing with the ERIES Data Portal can be easily preserved in the long-term publishing them as "Data Papers", i.e., peer-reviewed documents describing a data packet published in a peer-reviewed journal. Options in this direction include:
  1. EC's open-access publishing platform Open Research Europe (ORE), is offered as an additional publishing option to Horizon Europe beneficiaries, and if selected as a publishing venue, all requirements for open access to scientific publications are fulfilled, as ORE deposits publications in the all-purpose repository Zenodo.
  2. journals, like "Data in Brief" and "Journal of Structural Engineering (JSE)" of the American Society of Civil Engineering (ASCE)
In both cases, the described data packet must be publicly available through a digital object identifier (DOI) cited in the paper, and a link provided within the paper to ensure online access to the published data packet. Following the Grant Agreement (Section 1.2(f), page 163), TA users and facilities commit themselves - through the TA User Agreement they co-sign - to publish the research metadata as data articles (Data Papers) in persistent identifier-providing journals (e.g., Data in Brief journal, ASCE Journal of Structural Engineering, Open Research Europe, ORE).
Digital
https://www.zenodo.org/

Built and developed by researchers, to ensure that everyone can join in Open Science. The OpenAIRE project, in the vanguard of the open access and open data movements in Europe, was commissioned by the EC to support their nascent Open Data policy by providing a catch-all repository for EU-funded research. CERN, an OpenAIRE partner and pioneer in open source, open access and open data, provided this capability, and Zenodo was launched in May 2013. In support of its research programme, CERN has developed tools for Big Data management and extended Digital Library capabilities for Open Data. Through Zenodo, these Big Science tools could be effectively shared with the long­-tail of research.
https://about.gitlab.com/

GitLab is the DevOps platform that empowers organizations to maximize the overall return on software development by delivering software faster and efficiently while strengthening security and compliance. With GitLab, every team in your organization can collaboratively plan, build, secure, and deploy software to drive business outcomes faster with complete transparency, consistency and traceability.
GitLab is an open-core company which develops software for the software development lifecycle with 30 million estimated registered users and more than 1 million active license users and has an active community of more than 2,500 contributors.
National Infrastructures

A lab may opt for a National Infrastructure as a repository for its data, if existent and accessible. Data storage could be free-of-charge (at least for public entities, like Universities and Research Centers). In addition, longevity is assured.

The re-built Access Portal developed under WP3 of the ERIES project, will be storing a complete set of metadata describing the data of an experimental project. The metadata will be uploaded by ERIES facilities through a dedicated interface, with concurrent checking of the completeness of the metadata provided. For example, the DOI attributed to the data will have to be included in the metadata, alongside the link to the trusted repository where the data reside. The system will check the submission of a DOI and will not allow metadata to become "public" unless one is provided.
